why-version-control-matters.label: Why Version Control Matters
why-version-control-matters.summary: Understand the course prerequisites, the three cumulative repository journeys, and the inspect-change-verify habit.

sc-1.title: Purpose of Version Control
sc-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a version control system like Git?
sc-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Understand the problem Git solves before learning commands: one project, deliberate snapshots, and a history you can explain.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To manage changes and track project history
options.c: To increase the speed of development
options.d: To replace the need for project management tools

intro-memory.title: Before You Begin
intro-memory.bodyMarkdown: This course assumes only basic terminal navigation: you can recognize files and folders, use `pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `mkdir`, and `touch`, and open and save a plain-text file. You do **not** need programming, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GitHub, or previous Git experience. Whenever a file must change, the lesson supplies the exact path, exact text, and exact location.

worked-example.title: Three Small Projects, One Learning Pattern
worked-example.bodyMarkdown: The guided lessons grow `community-project/` across Modules 1–3. The module projects continue `volunteer-project/` in parallel. The capstone starts fresh in `neighborhood-guide/`. Each workspace stays intentionally small and mostly text-based so the Git state—not the file format—remains the lesson. Every journey follows **inspect → change → verify**.
